articles.title: IAVS-LAC section
articles.description: This website, managed by the Steering committee of the Latin America and the Carribean section of the International Association for Vegetation Science, will be used to share the most important information about our community"
articles.contents:
Purpose of the section
Historically, vegetation science from the region Latin America and the Carribean has been largely understudied, and much of the science comes from researchers from other regions.
The IAVS-LAC section aim at promoting vegetation science in the region, and to re-equilibrate the relationships between regional and global researchers.
Steering Committee
Lucrecia Lipoma



I am a Researcher at the Multidisciplinary Institute of Plant Biology (IMBIV–CONICET) and an Assistant Professor at the National University of Córdoba, Argentina. My research focuses on the mechanisms that underpin the resilience of plant communities, by combining local-scale experiments with global syntheses. Much of my work has focused on the Argentine Chaco, where I study ecosystem recovery following land-use change. I collaborate in interdisciplinary projects aimed at understanding ecosystem dynamics in the context of rapid ecological transformations, working closely with diverse social actors and stakeholders. Since 2016, I have been actively involved in the Latin American and Caribbean (LAC) Regional Section of the IAVS, helping to strengthen scientific exchange and collaboration throughout the region.
Mariana Dairel



I am a Post-Doc Researcher at Universidade Estadual Paulista (UNESP), Brazil. My research focuses on how fire shapes vegetation communities in the Cerrado, particularly how different fire regimes influence community assembly (composition, structure, and diversity), ecosystem functioning, and resilience in savanna ecosystems. Throughout my career, I have worked in fire ecology and savanna ecology, more recently incorporating the perspective of Integrated Fire Management (IFM). Over the years, I have been involved in conducting and participating in experimental prescribed burns for research purposes. This long-term experience has given me a solid understanding of how well-managed prescribed fire can support key ecological processes, reduce fuel loads, aid in the recovery of degraded areas, and contribute to safer and more functional landscapes. More recently, I have also become interested in contributing to the development of science-based public policies related to fire management and biodiversity conservation.
Marius Bottin



Currently working in the Instituto de Investigación de Recursos Biológicos Alexander von Humboldt in Bogotá, Colombia, I am a French ecologist who moved to Colombia in 2017. I worked mostly in numerical ecology and the use of large databases to study biogeographical patterns in various ecosystems. Now my job consists in managing biodiversity and socio-ecological datasets from the Humboldt institute and the data fluxes to create information platforms.
